coredata.dc:title: Construction of upconversion nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots modified BiVO4 photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Chunxia
Chen, Rongru
Ma, Changchang
Cheng, Rujia
Gao, Xin
Wang, Tao
Liu, Yang
Huo, Pengwei
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: A direct and facile “bottom-up’’ approach was carried out to prepare nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) under hydrothermal conditions. The as-synthesized NGQDs exhibited an excellent excitation-independent upconversion and downconversion photoluminescent (PL) behavior. And further, the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts were also fabricated to investigate enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance. The addition of NGQDs in the NGQDs/BiVO4 could improve the photocatalytic performance, and the results indicated 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composites showed the optimal photocatalytic performance. The quenching of effects of different scavengers demonstrated that superoxide radical played a major role in MB degradation. A possible mechanism that NGQDs could enhance the photocatalytic performance of NGQDs/BiVO4 composites had been also proposed. It was estimated that the photocatalytic performance of the NGQDs/BiVO4 composites for the degradation of MB under visible light could be primarily ascribed to upconversion PL character of NGQDs and the efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and holes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NGQDs
Upconversion
Photocatalysis
BiVO4
MB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a, b TEM image of as-prepared NGQDs; c size distribution of NGQDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the NGQDs dispersed in water; (b) PL spectra of the NGQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
Upconverted PL spectra of the NGQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
XRD patterns of the as-synthesized samples.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared samples; (b) Raman spectra of BiVO4 alone and 5.0 wt% NGQDs/ BiVO4 composite.
XPS spectra of the as-obtained 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 sample: (a) C 1s; (b) N 1s; (c) Bi 4f; (d) V2p; (e) O1s.
SEM images of as-synthesized BiVO4 (a) and 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4(b); (c) the corresponding EDS results of the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 sample; (d) TEM image of as-prepared 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 sample.

(a) Degradation dynamic curves; (b) UV–vis absorption spectral during the photocatalytic degradation of MB in the presence of 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite; (c) Cycling runs for the photocatalytic degradation of MB over the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4; (d) XRD patterns of the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 before and after photocatalysis.
(a) PL spectra; (b) transient photocurrent response; (c) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of as-synthesized samples.
Trapping experiments of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of MB over the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4.
Possible photocatalytic mechanism of the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite.

head.ce:title: Construction of upconversion nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots modified BiVO4 photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Fluid Machinery Engineering and Technology, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Fluid Machinery Engineering and Technology, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of physics, Jilin Normal University, Siping 136000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of physics, Jilin Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A direct and facile “bottom-up’’ approach was carried out to prepare nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) under hydrothermal conditions. The as-synthesized NGQDs exhibited an excellent excitation-independent upconversion and downconversion photoluminescent (PL) behavior. And further, the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts were also fabricated to investigate enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance. The addition of NGQDs in the NGQDs/BiVO4 could improve the photocatalytic performance, and the results indicated 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composites showed the optimal photocatalytic performance. The quenching of effects of different scavengers demonstrated that superoxide radical played a major role in MB degradation. A possible mechanism that NGQDs could enhance the photocatalytic performance of NGQDs/BiVO4 composites had been also proposed. It was estimated that the photocatalytic performance of the NGQDs/BiVO4 composites for the degradation of MB under visible light could be primarily ascribed to upconversion PL character of NGQDs and the efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and holes.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel kind of graphene-based nanomaterials with size less than 10 nm, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have currently aroused increasing attention on different fields since they were firstly reported on 2008 [1,2]. Interestingly, GQDs possess many outstanding physical properties such as superior photostability, excellent biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly nature. Because of these properties, GQDs are highly appropriate for solution-processable applications including biolimaging [3], fuel cells [4], biosensing [5], light emitting diodes [6], and photocatalysis [7]. Recently, some studies have shown that GQDs can effectively promote the activity of semiconductor photocatalysts, and GQDs/semiconductor nanocomposites have remarkable photocatalytic properties. For instance, Pan et al. coupled GQDs with TiO2 nanoparticles to achieve visible-light-driven photocatalyst GQDs sensitized TiO2 nanoparticles, which exhibits higher photocatalytic activity than using pure TiO2 nanoparticles photocatalysts [8]. Yin et al. reported that NGQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids possessed an enhanced photocatalytic activity for rhodamine B degradation to that of pure BiOBr nanohybrids [9]. According to Guo's report, the GQDs could be used as a green sensitizer to functionalize ZnO nanowire arrays on F-doped SnO2 glass, which promoted an enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting [10]. Owing to high electrical conductivity, GQDs may serve as an effective electron acceptor for semiconductor nanostructure, which facilitated charge carrier separation to polish up the photocatalytic efficiency. Furthermore, the high specific surface area of GQDs can increase the contact between the photocatalysts and reaction species, which can also improve the overall photocatalytic performance. Based on the above reasons, further interest could be stimulated in combining GQDs and semiconductor nanostruetrucs for photocatalysis applications.

On the other hand, with the rapid development of society, environmental contamination has become more and more serious. Consequently, lots of measures have been used to degrade contaminants, such as burying, reclamation, and incineration. In recent years, photo-degradation, with its non-pollution and low cost, has attracted comprehensive attention. In addition, Fe2O3, ZnO, TiO2, SnO2, BiVO4 Ag3PO4 have been reported to be applied as photocatalysts [11–15].

Among the known photocatalytic materials, BiVO4 plays an important role in the degradation of organic pollutants under visible light irradiation due to its narrow band gap and good crystallinity [16–19]. BiVO4 has a typical E
g of about 2.40 eV, exhibiting an intensive absorption in the visible region,and its response wanglength rangs up to 500 nm [20]. Nevertheless, BiVO4 faces two great flaws: the high-rate recombination of elecrton/hole (e-/h+) pairs and the poor visible light absorption efficiency [21–23]. Therefore, as well as GQDs' excellence in semiconductor-based photocatalytic applications, these challenges about BiVO4 motivate us whether we can logically formulate a novel class of GQDs/BiVO4 nanocomplex. In this combination of GQDs/BiVO4, some virtues can be obtained: (1) an amelioration of visible-light absortion capability; (2) the band gap can be well regulated through altering the photocatalyst composition; (3) charge transfer from BiVO4 nanoparticles to GQDs can lead to efficient charge separation. In addition, Doping can efficiently change the electronic density and tune transport and optical properties. According to Gong 's study, nitrogen doped graphene can heighten surface absorption of organic pollutants and O2 [24], which could degrade organic materials in a relatively short irradiation time. On account of the advantages of nitrogen-dopant in graphene nanostructrues, it can be calculated that the introduction of nitrogen into GQDs, namely NGQDs, will exhibit similar characters to graphene, which is of great importance both fundamentally and technologically. To the best of our knowledge, the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts have never been reported until now. as a result, NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts should be fabricated to future explore the possibility of improving the photocatalytic performance.

Herein, we presented a facile and green “bottom-up” methods to prepare NGQDs, which exhibited bright PL in a deinnized water solution. Interestingly, the as-prepared NGQDs displayed excitation-independent upconversion and downconversion PL properties. The PL spectra possessed a strong peak at around 440 nm. Significantly, we demonstrated the design of NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts prepared through a facile synthesis method. Subsequently, the photocatalytic performances of the NGQDs/BiVO4 composites were measured through degrading methylene blue (MB) under Xe lamp irradiation (λ > 420 nm). The experimental results showed the as-obtained NGQDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts exhibited more excellent photocatalytic activities than pure BiVO4 photocatalysts. Furthermore, the preliminary photocatalytic mechanism of the NGQDs/BiVO4 nanocomplex related to the band positions of the semiconductors was also discussed in detail.

All chenicals are commercial available and analysis pure grade without further purification. Citric acid monohydrate (CA), urea, ammonium vanadate (NH4VO3), bismuth nrtrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ammonium solution (NH3·H2O), nitric acid (HNO3), ethanol, methylene blue (MB), 1,4-benzoquione (BQ), n-butyl alcohol (NBA),disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na). Deionized water was used throughout the study.

NGQDs were synthesized through using CA as a carbon source and urea as a N source [25]. In a typical procedure, 0.105 g (0.5 mmol) CA and 0.09 g (1.5 mmol) urea were dissolved in 15 mL water followed by vigorous stirring for 30 min. Then, the clear solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the brown solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 36 h, producing a highly stable NGQDs suspension.

BiVO4 was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method as previously reported [26]. Typically, 2.425 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (5 mmol) were dissolved in 100 mL of 2.0 M nitric acid solutions. Then, 0.585 g NH4VO3 (5 mmol) were gradually added to this suspension under vigorous stirring. An orange precipitate was then obtained by adjusting the pH value of the above mixed solution to 2.1 by dropwise titration with ammonic solution under vigorous stirring. After aging for 2 h, the as-obtained orange product at the orange precipitate at the bottom of the beaker was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermal treated at 200 °C for 24 h. Finally, the obtained vivid yellow powder was collected and washed with deionized water and ethanol several times, and dried at 60 oC for overnight.

The NGQDs/BiVO4 photocatalysts were synthesized by a simple route. 0.2 g BiVO4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 10 mL deionized water. After that, a certain volume (5, 10, or 15 mL) of NGQDs (1 mg mL-1) solution was added to the above solution under stirring, forming a stable and homogeneous solution. Then, the mixed suspension was evaporated in water-bath conditions. Finally, the as-obtained products were further dried at 60 °C for 12 h. In addition, the added contents of NGQDs in the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite were controlled to 2.5 wt%, 5.0 wt% and 7.5 wt%, respectively, by simply controlling the weight ratio of NGQDs to BiVO4.

The crystal structures of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7001F) were used to detect the structural details and morphologies of the as-prepared samples. Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) was obtained by a PHI5300 (Pekin-Elmer PHI) instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS，Escalab 250Xi) was performed with a PHI5300 electron spectrometer to test the elements on the surface of complexes. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured on a Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450) using BaSO4 as the reference. Raman spectra was collected on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λexc= 633 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra was obtained by a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon Inc.) with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. The photocurrent and electronchemical impedance sperctroscopy (EIS) were conducted using a CHI660B electronchemical analyzer. Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis was performed on a Bruker ESR A 300-10/12 spectrometer to detect free radicals.

In order to compare the photocatalytic activity of pure BiVO 4 and NGQDs/BiVO4 nanocomplexes with different contents, the photocataly dgradation reaction was carried out through using MB as a model compound at room temperature. In the experiments, the MB solution (10 mg L-1, 100 mL) containing 50 mg of photocatalysts were added to a silica reaction glass. A 300 w Xe lamp was employed as a light source with a 420 nm cutoff filter to provide visible light irradiation. Prior to visible light illumination, the suspension was preserved from light and magnetically stirred for 30 min in the dark to achieve adsorption equilibrium. During irrdation, at regular intervals, 4 mL suspension was collected and centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 5 min) to remove the photocatalyst. The MB concentration was analyzed by a UV-2450 which recorded the intensity of the maximum band at 664 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra.

The active species were detected by using some sacrificial agents during photocatalytic reaction. In the experiments, these sacrificial agents, for instance, 1,4-benzoquione (BQ), iso-Propyl alcohol (IPA), and disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) were employed as a superoxide radical (O2 •−) scavenger, hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenger, and hole (h+) scavenger, respectively [27]. Except for the addition of 1 mM EDTA-2Na, 5 mM BQ and 5 mM IPA in the presence of MB, the experiment was identical with the former photocatalytic activity test.

The NGQDs were synthesized successfully using the bottom-up approaches. Fig. 1a and b show the TEM image of the NGQDs. The TEM images indicate that the size of the prepared NGQDs is more uniform. At the same time, the NGQDs have superior dispersivity and no agglomeration occurs. The diameters of the prepared NGQDs are mostly distributed in the range from 1.75 nm to 4.75 nm. In addition, the size distribution of the NGQDs shown in Fig. 1d indicates that the average size of prepared NGQDs is around 2.75 nm.

Fig. 2a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NGQDs in water. There are two clear absorption bands at 235 nm and 337 nm, which is in conformity with the previous reports [28–30]. These absorption bands are correlated with π→π* and n→π transition of C˭C and C˭O band in the NGQDs. In addition, in order to further investigate the optical properties of as-prepared NGQDs, a PL study was performed by using different excitation wavelengths. Generally speaking, when the excitation wavelength was a red shift, the PL peaks shifted to longer wavelengths with a maximum intensity. Nevertheless, the as-synthesized NGQDs in this study exhibit an excitation-independent PL feature. As shown in Fig. 2, with the excitation wavelength varies from 310 nm to 410 nm, the PL spectra is almost invariable at around 440 nm and show a strongest peak at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fig. 2b displays the photoluminescence spectrum of as-synthesized NGQDs.

Simultaneously, NGQDs remarkably exhibit a obvious upconversion PL feature. Fig. 3 shows the PL spectra of NGQDs excited by long-wavelength light with the upconverted emissions located at 440 nm, which is the same as the downconversion PL peak. Meanwhile, similar to the downconversion PL spectra, the upconversion PL also exhibit an excitation-independent PL behavior. The PL spectra is almost invariable and shows a strong peak at 440 nm with the excitation wavelength red shift (from 600 nm to 850 nm). The upconverted PL property of NGQDs should be attributed to the multiphoto active process similar to previous reported carbon dots [31,32].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out to show the purity and crystallinity of as-synthesized BiVO4 and NGQDs/BiVO4 with different NGQDs concents. As shown in Fig. 4, it can be obviously seen that the positions of all peaks can be indexed to the BiVO4 phase, which is consistent with the standard data (JCPDS no. 83-1700). At the same time, no characteristic diffraction peak of NGQDs is detected in NGQDs/BiVO4 samples, which may be attributed to the small amounts and low crystalinity of the NGQDs.

Herein, FT-IR and Raman spectra were employed to verify the successful loading of NGQDs. Fig. 5a shows the spectra of BiVO4 and NGQDs/BiVO4 with different NGQDs loading amounts. Compared with the spectra of BiVO4, additional FT-IR vibration peaks of the NGQDs in the NGQDs/BiVO4 composites are observed. The peak at 3443 cm-1 is attributed to the absorption peak of –OH whereas the peak at 3183 cm-1 is associated with the stretching vibration of C-H. The peak at 1601 cm-1 is assigned to the vibration of C˭C. It is clear that the stretching vibration of C-N at 1403 cm-1 can be observed, which may correspond with the successful adulteration of N atom. In addition, the FT-IR spectra exhibits a peak at 1197 cm-1, which is related to the stretching vibration of C-O. Meanwhile, the Raman spectra of the BiVO4 and 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite are illustrated in Fig. 5b. Compared with pure BiVO4, the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite represents two additional peaks at 1347 cm-1 and 1576 cm-1, corresponding to the D band and G band of NGQDs, respectively. Thus, these findings indicate that the NGQDs and BiVO4 have been coupled together successfully.

To study the chemical composition of NGQDs/BiVO4 complex, the XPS spectra of the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 is showed in Fig. 6. The C1 XPS spectra (Fig. 6a) clearly reveals three characteristic peaks, corresponding to C˭C at 284.6 eV, C-O at 286.8 eV and O-C˭O at 289.0 eV, respectively. Two peaks at 398.9 eV and 401.9 eV are assigned to pyrrolic-like N and N-H bonding of the NGQDs (Fig. 6b), respectively. XPS signals of Bi4f are observed at binding energies of 164.65 eV (Bi4f7/2) and 158.35 eV (Bi4f5/2) (Fig. 6c), ascribed to Bi3+. The V2p peak is centered at around 515.34 eV (Fig. 6d), corresponding to the signals of V5+ species. The peak at 531.8 eV in the O1s spectra (Fig. 6e) can be assigned to the lattice oxygen in the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite [16]. These results of XPS spectra are consistent with those of FT-IR spectra and further exhibit the existence of NGQDs in the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite.

The SEM images of as-synthesized BiVO4 and 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 samples are exhibited in Fig. 7a–b. There are no obvious differences in morphology between BiVO4 and 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4, which are both prepared with the lump-like shape. It can be clearly seen that the surface of BiVO4 is smooth whereas the surface of 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 is slight rough, which indicates, to a considerable extent, NGQDs is loaded onto the surface of lump-like BiVO4. In addition, there are small BiVO4 nanoparticles adhered to the surface of lump-like BiVO4, which may be attributed to high temperature (200 °C) and long heating-time (24 h) in the hydrothermal condition. Fig. 7c shows the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDS) element spectra of 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite. It is apparent that C, N, Bi, V, and O elements can be identified. Along with FT-IR, Raman, and XPS spectra, it is reasonable to confirm that NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts have been successfully synthesized. As shown in Fig. 7d, TEM image of the as-prepared 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 apparently exhibits that NGQDs are loaded onto the surface of BiVO4 photocatalysts as well.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectrace spectra (DRS) was used to measure pure BiVO4 and NGQDs/BiVO4 with different NGQDs contents in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. As shown in Fig. 8a, Compared to the pure BiVO4 sample, all the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite exhibits intense absorption in the visible region. Furthermore, with increasing the NGQDs contents from 2.5 wt% to 7.5 wt%, the absorption intensity in the range 520–800 nm of the NGQDs/ BiVO4 composites gradually increases, and the absorbance edges show marked red shifts, which eventually increases the exploitation of the solar spectra. Besides, the band gap energy (E
g ) of BiVO4 and 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 can be calculated through the following formula [33]:
(1)
α
E
p h o t o n
= K
(
E
p h o t o n
−
E
g
)
n / 2
where α, E
photon and K are the absorption coefficient, the discrete photoenergy and a constant for the material, respectively. Alongside these parameters, n is determined by the type of optical transition of a semiconductor (n = 2 for a direct transition and n = 4 for an indirect transition [34,35]). Fig. 8b represents plots for the relationship of (αE
photon )1/2 versus E
photon , which indicates that the band gaps of pure BiVO4 and 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite are calculated to be 2.36 eV and 2.13 eV, respectively. The lower band gap is, the better electronic transition and generation of active species are [36].

The photocatalytic performance of the as-synthesized samples was tested by photocatalytic degradation of MB under visible light irradiation. Fig. 9a exhibits the photocatalytic performances of NGQDs/BiVO4 composite with different NGQDs content. As shown in Fig. 9a, when no photocatalysts is added, the photodegradation rate of MB is extraordinarily slow. In the presence of photocatalysts, the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite exhibits much higher photocatalytic activity than that of the pure BiVO4 sample. Among the different NGQDs/BiVO4 composites, 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite has the highest photocatalytic activity. After the irradiation for 200 min, nearly 90% of MB are photodegraded by 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite while 60% of MB disappeared by the use of pure BiVO4 samples. This result shows the appropriate introduction of NGQDs can be make improvement of photocatalytic performance with BiVO4 samples. Fig. 9b displays the time-dependent absorption spectra of MB solution in the presence of 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite. Apparent decreases of MB absorption at 664 nm are observed, after 200 min of irradiation, about 90% removal of MB is achieved. In addition, the cycling experiment for the photodegradation of MB in the presence of 5.0 wt% NGQDs/ BiVO4 composite was also carried out and exhibited in Fig. 9c. The result indicates that after five catalytic runs, the photodecomposition rate of MB still remains almost unchanged. Fig. 9d shows the XRD patterns of the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite before and after the photocatalytic reaction. After 3 cycles, the positions and rations of the peaks are almost the same as those of the original photocatalyst. Consequently, the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite described in this paper not only exhibits superior photocatalytic properties, also has extraordinary stability during the photocatalytic reaction, indicating the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite could be applied in the field of photochemical catalysis.

The PL spectra of as-obtained photocatalysts were performed to make a comparison under an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. As exhibited in Fig. 10a, it can be obviously observed that the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite shows the lowest PL intensity. This result corresponds to the experimental result of photocatalytic performance of the as-synthesized samples and indicates the effective separation of photo-generated electrons and holes [37], which may be a reason for the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite exhibiting enhanced photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation. Consistent with the UV–vis DRS, the introduction of NGQDs could increase the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and improve the absorption efficiency to enhance the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts. The photocurrent and EIS measurements were used to further investigate the transfer and recombination process of electron-hole pairs. As is known to all, if the photocurrent is higher, separation efficiency of the electrons and holes will be better and hence the photocatalytic activity is more advantageous [38]. The transient photocurrent responses of as-prepared BiVO4 and 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 were recorded for several on-off cycles under visible light irradiation (Fig. 10b). The photocurrent of the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 material is 2.1 times higher than that of the pure BiVO4 material. The photocurrent enhancement of 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 indicates an enhanced photoinduced electron and hole separation. As shown in Fig. 10c, it can be found that the electron-transfer resistance of 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite is smaller than that of the pure BiVO4, indicating that there exist more effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes and more fast interfacial charge transfer after NGQDs were coupled with BiVO4 [39]. Based on good charge-carrier-transfer behavior and enhanced photocurrent intensity, NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts could be photoactive for the degradation of organic pollutants.

It is of great importance to explore the photocatalytic mechemism of the as-prepared NGQDs/BiVO4 composite in the photodegradation process. Usually, the photogenerated holes and radicals are the main species in the MB photocatalytic degradation process. And hence in the trapping experiments, BQ, NBA and EDTA-2Na were used to be a quencher of superoxide radical (O2 •−), hydroxyl radical (•OH) and hole (h+), respectively [26]. As revealed in Fig. 11, under visible-light irradiation, the 5.0 wt% NGQDs/BiVO4 composite exhibits a slight decrease of the photocatalytic activity after the addition of hydroxyl radical quencher NBA and hole quencher EDTA-2Na, indicating the hydroxyl radicals and holes are not the main active species responsible for the degradation of MB in these photocatalytic systems. However, it is interesting to find the addition of BQ (superoxide radical quencher) can significantly inhibit the MB photodegradation. These results show the active species O2 •− radicals exert a major influence on MB degradation over the NGQDs/BiVO4 composites under visible light illumination.

At the same time, in order to illustrate the improvement process of the photocatalytic performance of the composite materials, the band position was calculated according to a previous paper [40] as follows:
(2)
E
C B
= X −
E
e
− 0.5
E
g
(3)
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E
g
where X is the electronegativity of the semiconductor (X for BiVO4 is 6.04) and E
e is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV). According to the UV–vis DRS, the band gap of BiVO4 is 2.36 eV. Meanwhile, the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of BiVO4 could be calculated to be 0.35 eV and 2.71 eV, respectively.

Based on the above results in this system, a possible mechemism of the MB photocatalytic degradation process over NGQDs/BiVO4 composites is proposed in Fig. 12. The NGQDs in NGQDs/BiVO4 composite plays dual important roles in the improved photocatalytic activity. Under visible light irradiation, the upconverted PL property of incorporated NGQDs can absorb the long wavelength light and emit shorter wavelength light, which subsequently excites BiVO4 to generate electron-hole pairs [41,42]. Simultaneously, the photogenerated electrons are excited from the VB of BiVO4 to the CB. Importantly, NGQDs also serve as the electron reservoirs to trap these electrons, which results in the efficient separation of electrons and holes and prolong the lifetime of charges, thus increasing photocatalytic performance. In addition, the electrons generated from sunlight irradiation can react with O2 adsorbed on the surfaces of the NGQDs to generate O2 •− radicals, and the photoexcited holes in the VB of BiVO4 can oxidize H2O to give •OH, eventually leading to effective degradation of the MB dye.

In summary, NGQDs with excellent PL properties were successfully synthesized through a direct and facile “bottom-up’’ methods under hydrothermal conditions. The as-prepared NGQDs exhibited an extraordinary excitation-independent upconversion and downconversion PL behavior. Then, the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts were also designed to show an enhanced visible-light photoactivity. The amount of the NGQDs had a significant influence on the photoactivity of NGQDs/BiVO4 composite, and the optimal starting mass radio of NGQDs to BiVO4 was 0.05. The enhanced photoactivity of NGQDs/BiVO4 composite could be attributed to upconversion PL character of NGQDs, better optical absorption, lower recombination rate of the electron-hole pairs and smaller resistance. Besides, the NGQDs/BiVO4 composite photocatalysts demonstrated a high photostability after three cycles under visible-light irradiation without significant charge in the photocatalytic degradation of MB. The active species superoxide radical (O2 •−) played an important role in this system. Furthermore, the possible pathway of NGQDs on the enhancement photocatalytic activity of NGQDs/BiVO4 composite had been proposed. This work indicated the NGQDs sensitized photocatalysts are a promising photocatalytic material that has good potential application to pollutant purification.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510126, 21676115, U1662125), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20180884), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2017M621641, 2014M561595, 2015M571684).
